retrieved_docs.title: Rattlesnake Toxicity -- Treatment / Management -- Crotalidae Polyvalent Immune Fab Dosing
retrieved_docs.content: Crotalidae Polyvalent Immune Fab is derived from 4 snake species (Western Diamondback, Eastern Diamondback, Mojave rattlesnake, and Cottonmouth) and immunized into sheep (ovine-derived). The whole immunoglobin is extracted, affinity purified, and cleaved by papain into the terminal Fab fragment of the immunoglobin. This reduces its size by about 1/3 and allows tissue penetration. However, it is cleared renally, and repeat dosing is usually necessary.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Blood Brain Barrier -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: As previously discussed, the BBB is relatively impermeable under normal physiologic conditions. However, in pathologic conditions, several chemical mediators that increase BBB permeability may be released, including glutamate, aspartate, taurine, ATP, endothelin-1, ATP, NO, MIP-2, TNF-alpha, and IL-beta, which are produced by astrocytes. Other agents that increase BBB permeability include bradykinin, 5HT, histamine, thrombin, UTP, UMP, substance P, quinolinic acid, platelet-activating factor, and free radicals.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Blood Brain Barrier -- Other Issues -- Current Strategies for Delivery of Drugs Across the BBB
retrieved_docs.content: As it is the tightest endothelium in the body, the BBB represents the main impediment to drug delivery to the brain. It has been estimated that greater than 90% of all small-molecule drugs and close to 100% of all larger therapeutics cannot overcome the BBB. [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Herbal Supplements -- Function -- Garlic
retrieved_docs.content: Garlic ( Allium sativum ) is among the most researched herbal supplements and is the second most used complementary therapy. [3] In the United States, garlic has primarily been used to reduce hypercholesterolemia and hypertension. Studies have shown that garlic has hepatoprotective, neuroprotective, and antioxidant properties. In several studies, S-allyl cysteine, a compound found in garlic, demonstrated neuroprotective and cardioprotective properties by inhibiting cellβ damage in the heart, neurons, and endothelium. [4] S-allyl cysteine has also been shown to destabilize amyloid - β  fibrils found in Alzheimer disease. [5] S-allyl cysteine inhibits free radical production, neuronal damage, and lipid peroxidation in rats with brain ischemia. In severely hypertensive patients, garlic has been shown to reduce blood pressure and cardiovascular events. S-propyl-L-cysteine, a structural analog of S-allyl cysteine, has been shown to reverse gastric cancer in mice. [6] Allicin is a compound in garlic produced when it is chopped or crushed. A daily dose of 0.5 to 1.5 g of allicin significantly reduced HbA1c levels in patients with type 2 diabetes within 12 weeks. [7] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Cromolyn Sodium -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Cromolyn sodium is absorbed poorly via oral solution and has a delayed onset of action of 2 to 6 weeks after initiation. Similarly, less than 0.07% of administered cromolyn sodium is absorbed from ophthalmic solution or drops. The effect of cromolyn sodium on mast cells lasts approximately 6 hours following administration. The bioavailability of cromolyn sodium is 0.5% to 2%, with a half-life of 80 to 90 minutes. Cromolyn sodium is primarily excreted (98%) in the feces unabsorbed, with the remainder excreted in the urine.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 1434
retrieved_docs.content: Ibopamine and crofelemer are both classified as substances with a phenol structure, indicating that each compound contains the phenolic chemical motif that can affect its pharmacologic behavior. Crofelemer, in particular, is formulated as the active ingredient of the medication crofelemer 125 mg delayed‑release oral tablet marketed under the name Mytesi. This connection underscores that the phenolic nature of crofelemer is leveraged in a specific delayed‑release tablet dosage form to deliver its therapeutic effect.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 633
retrieved_docs.content: Protective and Adsorbent Drugs includes titanium dioxide as a subordinate component, reflecting the use of this inorganic compound as an excipient that provides protective and adsorbent properties in pharmaceutical formulations. Crofelemer serves as the active ingredient of the crofelemer 125 MG delayed‑release oral tablet marketed as Mytesi, while titanium dioxide is present in the same tablet as an inactive ingredient, consistent with its classification under Protective and Adsorbent Drugs. Both crofelemer and the clay mineral attapulgite exert a physiologic effect of stimulating large‑intestine fluid and electrolyte absorption, a mechanism that underlies their therapeutic role in managing secretory diarrhoea. The integration of crofelemer’s active pharmacologic action with titanium dioxide’s protective excipient function results in a delayed‑release oral product designed to deliver the drug effectively to the gastrointestinal tract while maintaining tablet stability and integrity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 1972
retrieved_docs.content: Bioflavonoid drugs for capillary stabilizing are classified as capillary stabilizing agents and are directly associated with bioflavonoids. As a type of capillary stabilizing agent, these drugs leverage the properties of bioflavonoids to reinforce capillary wall integrity, reduce permeability, and prevent hemorrhagic complications. Their relationship with bioflavonoids underscores the therapeutic role of flavonoid compounds in vascular health, making them relevant for conditions where capillary fragility is a concern.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 939
retrieved_docs.content: Crofelemer is classified as a substance that possesses a phenol structure, situating it within the broader category of phenolic compounds and underscoring the chemical characteristics that influence its pharmacologic properties.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1361
retrieved_docs.content: Crofelemer is categorized in the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) classification as a member of the “other antidiarrheals” group, indicating its therapeutic role in treating diarrhea. Chemically, crofelemer belongs to the class of substances that possess a phenol structure, linking its pharmacologic activity to the properties of phenolic compounds. This dual characterization—both as an antidiarrheal agent within the ATC system and as a phenolic substance—provides insight into its mechanism of action and informs its use in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 1814
retrieved_docs.content: Crofelemer 125 mg delayed‑release oral tablet, marketed as Mytesi, contains the active pharmaceutical ingredient crofelemer. Recognizing that crofelemer is the drug’s active component clarifies the tablet’s composition and informs appropriate prescribing and patient counseling.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 2 -- Community 437
retrieved_docs.content: Bioflavonoid drugs for capillary stabilizing are identified as bioflavonoidy and are classified as capillary stabilizing agents, indicating that they belong to the broader group of medications used to reinforce capillary integrity and reduce vascular permeability. In the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) classification, other capillary stabilizing agents in ATC are also categorized as capillary stabilizing agents, underscoring that both the bioflavonoid‑based products and the additional agents share the same therapeutic purpose of supporting capillary health. This relationship highlights the role of bioflavonoid compounds within the larger class of capillary stabilizing agents and situates them alongside other pharmacologic options that aim to maintain microvascular stability in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.content: members of **Bismuth preparation**, which is a child of **Protective and Adsorbent Drugs** (the latter also includes **taniny** and **titanium dioxide**). Additional intestinal adsorbents are listed under **Other intestinal adsorbents in ATC**, exemplified by **attapulgite**, and under **Charcoal preparations** (intestinal absorbents) exemplified by **aktivní uhlí** (active charcoal). **Crofelemer** is classified in **OTHER ANTIDIARRHEALS** and originates from **proantokyanidiny** (proanthocyanidins), a phenol‑structured class whose parent is **taniny**; crofelemer is the active ingredient of the oral tablet **crofelemer 125 MG Delayed Release Oral Tablet (Mytesi)**, which also contains **titanium dioxide** as an inactive ingredient. Both crofelemer and attapulgite produce the physiological effect **Stimulation of Large Intestine Fluid/Electrolyte Absorption**, highlighting their role in modulating intestinal fluid and electrolyte balance. The category **CARBOHYDRATE/ELECTROLYTES** is

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1401
retrieved_docs.content: Crofelemer 125 mg delayed‑release oral tablet (Mytesi) contains titanium dioxide as an inactive ingredient, indicating that titanium dioxide is used as an excipient in the formulation of this medication.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 1 -- Community 49
retrieved_docs.content: The therapeutic category “ANTIDIARRHEALS, INTESTINAL ANTIINFLAMMATORY/ANTIINFECTIVE AGENTS” is organized into four subgroups: antidiarrheal microorganisms (e.g., Saccharomyces boulardii), antipropulsives (opioid‑derived agents that reduce intestinal motility), intestinal adsorbents, and “other antidiarrheals in ATC.” Intestinal adsorbents include bismuth preparations, charcoal preparations, other intestinal adsorbents in ATC, attapulgite, and methylated pectin. Bismuth preparations belong to the broader “Protective and Adsorbent Drugs” class, contain bismuth compounds such as bismuth subgallate, bismuth subsalicylate, and the combination bismuth biskalcitrate + metronidazole + tetracycline HCl, and use excipients such as taniny and titanium dioxide. Crofelemer, classified under “other antidiarrheals in ATC,” is the active ingredient of the delayed‑release tablet Mytesi (125 mg); it is derived from proanthocyanidins (tannins as parent compounds) and promotes fluid and electrolyte absorption in the large intestine. Attapulgite, a mineral clay, shares this physiologic effect and is listed in the ATC class “Other intestinal adsorbents.” Methylated pectin, also in this ATC class, serves as an inactive ingredient in cholestyramine resin powder for oral suspension. Both Mytesi and a folic‑acid/polysaccharide‑iron/vitamin B12 oral capsule contain titanium dioxide as a filler excipient. Additionally, ibopamine is a child of the “Substance with phenol structure” class, indicating its phenolic chemical nature. Together, these entities illustrate a hierarchical framework linking antidiarrheal pharmacologic subclasses, specific agents, their mechanisms (opioid‑mediated motility reduction, adsorbent‑mediated fluid balance), and supporting excipients within the broader context of protective and adsorbent drug therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 754
retrieved_docs.content: Titanium dioxide is employed as an inactive ingredient in both the oral capsule formulation containing folic acid 1 mg, polysaccharide iron complex 150 mg, and vitamin B12 0.025 mg, and in the crofelemer 125 mg delayed‑release oral tablet marketed as Mytesi. In each product, titanium dioxide functions as an excipient that contributes to the tablet or capsule’s physical characteristics without providing therapeutic effect, and its presence may be relevant for patients with sensitivities to this additive.
